stories.title: Charleston QF Day — Pegula Into the Semifinal, Keys-Bencic Showdown [TIER 1]

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text: Defending champion Jessica Pegula survives another marathon at Charleston and is into the semifinal. Three consecutive grind wins on green clay. She's earning this defense the hard way. 🎾 #TennisFanrecap #CharlestonOpen #WTA

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text: Keys vs. Bencic in the Charleston QF: 2025 Australian Open champion vs. 2016 Charleston champion. Two former Top 5 players. One semifinal spot. This is the matchup of the week on green clay. 🏆 #TennisFanrecap #CharlestonOpen #WTA

facebook.long_form:
```
Friday was quarterfinal day in Charleston, and the green clay delivered exactly what it always promises: nothing is easy, and everyone has to earn it.

Jessica Pegula — the defending champion, the No. 1 seed, the player this whole tournament is built around — had to fight through yet another marathon to reach the semifinal. The Post and Courier called it "yet another thriller." That's three consecutive hard matches for Pegula this week. She's not cruising. She's grinding. And she keeps advancing.

That's actually a better story than easy wins would be. Charleston's green clay is the only surface like it on the WTA Tour — lower bounce, faster conditions, longer rallies. It doesn't care about your ranking. It makes you work for every point, every game, every set. Pegula is getting a masterclass in what defending a title on this surface actually requires.

The marquee QF was Keys vs. Bencic: the 2025 Australian Open champion against the 2016 Charleston champion. Madison Keys has been in commanding form all week. Belinda Bencic has been one of the tournament's signature storylines — the Olympic gold medalist making her comeback from maternity leave, back on a surface where she knows how to win. Both players deserve a Charleston semifinal. One of them isn't getting one.

Friday's QF day also featured Iva Jovic — the teenager who has been charming the Charleston crowd all week — taking on Anna Kalinskaya for another SF spot.

Green clay has delivered all week. The semifinals are going to be worth waking up for.

What was your favorite moment from Charleston's quarterfinals?
```
facebook.image_caption:
#### Image Caption -- 11:00 AM ET

```
Pegula grinds into the Charleston SF. Keys vs. Bencic in the QF of the week. Green clay has no off days.
```

stories.title: Monte Carlo Draw Ceremony — Alcaraz vs. Sinner, the 1,000-Point Battle Begins [TIER 1]

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text: Monte Carlo draw ceremony is TODAY at 5 PM CEST. Alcaraz has 1,000 points to defend. Sinner has zero. One bracket reveal changes the entire No. 1 race. Clay season's biggest day. 🎾 #TennisFanrecap #MonteCarlo #ATP

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text: Tsitsipas has won Monte Carlo THREE times (2021, 2022, 2024) and enters 2026 unseeded. Whoever draws him in the first round is not happy about it. The Monte Carlo bracket danger nobody is talking about. #TennisFanrecap #MonteCarlo #ATP

facebook.long_form:
```
At 5:00 PM today in Monaco, they pull the balls from the draw bag for the Rolex Monte-Carlo Masters — and the implications for the ATP No. 1 race are almost impossible to overstate.

Carlos Alcaraz walks in as the defending champion with 1,000 ranking points to protect. Jannik Sinner walks in with zero points to defend — he missed the 2025 event during his suspension. Sinner just won the Miami Open without dropping a set, the first man to complete the Sunshine Double (Indian Wells + Miami, no set dropped) since Roger Federer in 2017. He arrives in Monaco on the best form of his life with nothing to lose.

That's the framework for this draw. If Alcaraz wins Monte Carlo, he extends his lead over Sinner. If Alcaraz loses early — even in the second round — Sinner could end the tournament as the new world No. 1. The bracket reveal today doesn't just preview a great tennis week. It sets the stakes for the entire clay season.

Five top players withdrew before the draw: Novak Djokovic, Holger Rune, Taylor Fritz, Jack Draper, and Sebastian Korda. The field is more open than anyone expected a month ago. The wild cards — Moise Kouamé (17), Gaël Monfils (39), Matteo Berrettini (29), and Stan Wawrinka (42) — add generational breadth to a draw already full of storylines.

And then there's Stefanos Tsitsipas: a three-time Monte Carlo champion (2021, 2022, 2024), entering unseeded. He knows this court better than anyone not named Alcaraz. Wherever he lands in the draw, someone has a problem.

Qualifying starts tomorrow, April 4. Main draw play begins Sunday, April 5.

Who do you think wins Monte Carlo 2026?
```

#### Image Caption -- 8:30 AM ET

```
Monte Carlo draw is TODAY. Alcaraz defends 1,000 points. Sinner defends zero. The clay season's biggest bracket reveal.
```

stories.title: Keys vs. Bencic — A Charleston QF for the Ages [TIER 2]

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text: Keys beats Bencic OR Bencic beats Keys — either way we had two former champions on a Charleston QF day that delivered everything green clay can offer. The best individual matchup of the tournament. 🎾 #TennisFanrecap #CharlestonOpen #WTA

facebook.long_form:
```
The quarterfinal nobody wanted to see end was Madison Keys vs. Belinda Bencic at the Credit One Charleston Open — and the reason for that is simple: both of them deserved to win.

Madison Keys entered Friday's QF on a clean week. She beat Anna Bondar 6-2, 7-5 in the round of 16 — decisive, authoritative tennis from a player who won the 2025 Australian Open and has the kind of flat strike that Charleston's green clay actually rewards. Keys has never won Charleston, but her game is built for this surface. She was not here to make up the numbers.

Belinda Bencic brings something different. She won Charleston in 2016. She is the Paris 2024 Olympic gold medalist. She took a break from the tour to start a family and came back — and then kept winning. Her flat groundstrokes, her net instincts, her ability to change pace and direction — all of it is calibrated to green clay in a way that very few players on tour can match. Coming back to Charleston as a former champion is not nostalgia. It's legitimacy.

Two former champions. Two different kinds of form. One green-clay quarterfinal.

This is what tennis is supposed to look like.

Who were you rooting for in this one?
```

```

stories.title: Marrakech — Darderi Gets Free Semifinal Pass, Van Assche Into QF [TIER 2]

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text: Luciano Darderi — defending Marrakech champion — advances to the semifinal without hitting a ball. Hanfmann withdraws before their QF. Darderi is the freshest player left in the draw. #TennisFanrecap #Marrakech #ATP

facebook.long_form:
```
Luciano Darderi did not hit a ball on Friday, and he's in the semifinals at Marrakech.

The defending Grand Prix Hassan II champion received a quarterfinal walkover when Yannick Hanfmann withdrew before their scheduled match. It's one of the more unusual paths to a clay-court semifinal you'll see this week — but that's the nature of a deep tournament schedule on a clay surface that punishes the body.

Darderi will enter Saturday's semifinal as the freshest player in the draw. He didn't burn energy on a three-set QF. He didn't extend a muscle or test a knee. Whoever faces him in the semis is playing a rested, motivated defending champion.

The more interesting story out of Friday's Marrakech action is Luca Van Assche. The young French player beat No. 2 seed Tallon Griekspoor 6-4, 1-6, 6-3 earlier in the tournament — one of the week's cleaner upsets on Moroccan red clay. Van Assche now plays his quarterfinal against Carabelli to try to join Darderi in Saturday's semis.

Marrakech doesn't have the spotlight that Monte Carlo will demand next week. But it's producing real tennis from players who are genuinely fighting for something. Van Assche, in particular, is a name worth knowing before the clay season gets deeper.

Are you following the Marrakech draw this week?
```
facebook.image_caption:
#### Image Caption -- 6:30 PM ET

```
Darderi walks into the Marrakech SF. Van Assche earned his spot the hard way. Red clay, real stakes.
```

stories.title: Jovic Makes Charleston QF — Teen Sensation Faces Kalinskaya [TIER 3]

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text: Iva Jovic — teenager, No. 4 seed — is in the Charleston quarterfinals after beating Sofia Kenin 7-5, 7-5. She now faces Kalinskaya for a spot in the SF. The WTA's next generation is not waiting its turn. 🌟 #TennisFanrecap #CharlestonOpen #WTA

facebook.long_form:
```
The story of this Charleston Open — before the semifinals, before the final, before whoever lifts the trophy — is Iva Jovic.

The teenager walked into this tournament as the No. 4 seed and has done nothing but make it look like she belongs. This week she beat Sofia Kenin 7-5, 7-5 in the round of 16 — a solid, experienced player who has a Grand Slam title to her name. It wasn't a fluke. It was two competitive sets, won cleanly, by a player who kept competing at every big moment.

That match got Jovic to the quarterfinals, where she faced Anna Kalinskaya — the No. 8 seed who beat Paula Badosa to get there. Kalinskaya is aggressive, experienced, and not here to hand a teenager a milestone moment. This was Jovic's biggest test of the week.

What's made this run special isn't just the results. It's the way she's gone about it. The Post and Courier noted it in their coverage last week: "Teen tennis star charms Charleston Open crowd." That doesn't happen by accident. It happens when a player is playing with real joy, real ambition, and an instinct for the moment.

Charleston's green clay is the perfect stage for the WTA's next generation. Jovic has made the most of it.

What do you think of Jovic's week in Charleston?
```

```
